text: Following bilateral uterine artery coagulation, the surgeon inserts a myoma screw and uses a monopolar device to make a sharp incision at the most prominent part of the fibroid along the posterior uterine wall.
The surgeon uses a monopolar instrument to enucleate a deep intramural fibroid from its bed, carefully dissecting the fibroid out of its capsule.
The surgeon applies gentle traction and counter traction with the myoma screw inserted, using a monopolar instrument to continue the dissection and enucleate the fibroid from its vascular bed.

The surgeon uses a monopolar device to carefully remove the fine attachments of the fibroid from its vascular bed. Due to prior bilateral uterine artery coagulation, there is minimal bleeding visible throughout the procedure.
The surgeon sutures the fibroid capsule back in multiple layers using a barbed suture, which allows for knotless closure and expedites the surgical process.
The surgeon places deep interrupted sutures within the myoma cavity using barbed sutures, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
Using a suprapubic port and two needle holders, the surgeon takes deep bites through the myoma bed, pulling the suture and applying gentle traction to close the cavity and prevent hematoma formation.
The surgeon observes minimal bleeding from the vascular bed due to prior uterine artery coagulation. Continuous sutures are applied using a barbed suture to close the cavity completely.
The surgeon uses a monopolar device to make a sharp cut at the level of the myoma, ensuring precise approximation of the uterine serosa and enhancing the quality of uterine healing.
After completing the suturing, the surgeon uses a morcellator to extract the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the myoma bed using barbed sutures, adding an extra stitch to interlock with previous stitches, ensuring consistent tension along the suture line.
The surgeon enucleates the fibroid from the uterine cavity, using the opposite port as a guide to form long strips of tissue, which are then extracted from the peritoneal cavity.
